1: Hiram king of Tyre’s embassy marks Tyre’s emergence as the leading Phoenician city-state. Cedar from Lebanon was the prestige building material of the ancient Near East — used in Egyptian temples, Mesopotamian palaces, and later Solomon’s temple. Tyre controlled the timber trade from the Lebanese mountains. Hiram’s sending craftsmen (ḥārāšê ʿēṣ, carpenters/woodworkers) alongside materials reflects the patron-client diplomacy of the period: great kings signaled recognition by material support for palace-building.
2: ‘David perceived (wayyadaʿ) that the LORD had established him as king’ — the verb yādaʿ here means covenantal/experiential knowledge, not merely intellectual recognition. Hiram’s embassy functioned as external confirmation of divine election. The phrase ‘his kingdom was highly exalted for the sake of his people Israel’ is significant: Davidic elevation serves a theocratic purpose — the king’s greatness is for the people’s sake, not his own glory.
3: The listing of multiple wives and children in Jerusalem reflects ANE royal practice: a large household demonstrated the king’s status, and alliances were often sealed through marriage. The Chronicler lists this without the negative evaluation that Deuteronomy 17:17 implies (‘he must not take many wives’). The focus is dynastic expansion; the sons listed will become the ancestors of royal lines tracked through Chronicles.
4: Shammua, Shobab, Nathan, Solomon — the first four sons of David’s Jerusalem wives. Nathan (v4) is significant: he appears in Luke 3:31 as the ancestor of Mary’s lineage of Jesus, while Matthew 1:6 traces Joseph’s lineage through Solomon. The two Messianic genealogies in the NT diverge here, at David’s sons, which Chronicles places at the founding of Jerusalem-based royalty.
5: Ibhar (‘he chooses’), Elishua (‘my God is salvation’), and Elpelet (‘God of deliverance’) — three sons with theophoric names reflecting the theology of the Davidic court. Such names (composed with ʾēl) signal the religious orientation of the household; they are common in the Jerusalem-period onomasticon.
6: Nogah, Nepheg, and Japhia — three more sons. The list of thirteen sons at vv4-7 (parallel to 2 Samuel 5:14-16) establishes the Davidic dynasty’s breadth in Jerusalem. The Chronicler repeats this list from 1 Chronicles 3:5-8, weaving dynastic continuity through the narrative. Large royal households in the ANE were both a sign of status and a political reality: each son represented a potential alliance or succession claim.
7: Elishama, Beeliada, and Eliphelet. Beʿelyāḏāʿ (‘Baal knows’) retains the Ba’al theophoric element here in Chronicles, while 2 Samuel 5:16 reads ‘Eliada’ (ʾelyāḏāʿ, ‘God knows’). The scribal substitution of ʾēl for baʿal in Samuel reflects the later aversion to Ba’al names after the prophets’ campaigns against Ba’al worship. The Chronicler’s retention of the original form shows either access to an older source or a more historically precise tradition.
8: The Philistines’ response to David’s anointing over all Israel reflects their prior political relationship. David had lived among the Philistines as a vassal of Achish of Gath (1 Sam 27; 29) — they knew him as a subject. His elevation to king of a united Israel ended that arrangement and threatened Philistine dominance of the coastal plain. ‘All the Philistines went up to search for David’ — a punitive military expedition against a former vassal who had escaped their control.
9: The Valley of Rephaim (southwest of Jerusalem, near Bethlehem) was a strategic corridor between the Judean hills and the coastal plain. ‘Rephaim’ (ancient giants) gave their name to this valley (Genesis 15:20; Deuteronomy 2:11, 20); the term carried connotations of pre-Israelite peoples of unusual stature. The valley appears in Isaiah 17:5 as agricultural land; militarily, it provided an open approach toward Jerusalem from the southwest.
10: ‘David inquired of God (šāʾal dāwid bē’lōhîm)’ is the Chronicler’s most important success formula. The verb šāʾal (ask, inquire) and dāraš (seek, inquire) together define the proper relationship between king and YHWH. Saul’s defining failure was that ‘he did not inquire of the LORD’ (1 Chronicles 10:14). David’s consistent inquiry before battle explains his military success — not strategy or strength but divine consultation. YHWH’s response: ‘Go up, for I will give them into your hand.’
11: Baʿal Pĕrāṣîm (‘Lord/master of the breakthroughs’) — the site name commemorates YHWH’s victory. David’s declaration ‘God has broken through (pāraṣ) my enemies by my hand, like a breakthrough of waters’ names YHWH as baʿal (master/lord) of the pereṣ (breach). The term pāraṣ connects to Pereṣ-ʿuzzāʾ (1 Chronicles 13:11): the same divine breakout that killed Uzza for improper approach now destroys Israel’s enemies. The direction of divine force depends on proper alignment with YHWH’s will.
12: The Philistines abandoned their gods on the battlefield — a reversal of the ark capture (1 Sam 5: Philistines took Israel’s sacred object). Now Israel burns the Philistines’ idols, following Deuteronomy 7:5 and 1 Chronicles 12:3 (‘burn their idols with fire’). The Chronicler’s version says ‘burned with fire’ where 2 Samuel 5:21 says ‘David and his men carried them away’ — Chronicles conforms the action to Deuteronomic law, perhaps idealizing the event or preserving a different tradition.
13: A second Philistine incursion into the same valley. The Philistines’ persistence is a narrative setup for demonstrating that David did not rely on the first victory’s momentum but again sought divine guidance. Each military campaign is treated as a new occasion for inquiry — the principle being not tactical self-reliance based on past success but renewed dependence on YHWH’s specific direction.
14: ‘Do not go up after them; go around behind them and come against them opposite the balsam trees’ — YHWH gives a different tactical instruction than in the first battle (there: direct assault; here: flanking maneuver with a specific signal). The variation emphasizes that divine guidance is not a formula but specific direction requiring attention. The balsam/mulberry trees (bĕkāʾîm) provided cover for the flanking approach and a visible signal point.
15: ‘When you hear the sound of marching (qôl haṣṣĕʿādâ) in the tops of the balsam trees, then go out to battle’ — the sound in the treetops signals divine movement. The ‘marching’ imagery evokes the divine warrior going before the army (cf. Judges 4:14: ‘the LORD has gone out before you’; Joshua 10:14). YHWH acts independently and the human army follows the divine lead. The specific natural signal (wind in trees?) was both practically useful and theologically significant.
16: ‘David did as God commanded (ṣiwwâ)’ — obedience to the specific divine word produces the specific result. From Gibeon (northwest of Jerusalem) to Gezer (on the coastal plain, at the entrance to the Shephelah) — a broad sweep that cleared the Philistine threat from the hill country approaches. Gezer was a major Canaanite city that would not be fully incorporated until Solomon’s time (1 Kings 9:16-17).
17: ‘The LORD brought the fear (pāḥad) of him upon all nations’ — pāḥad (terror, dread) echoes the covenant promises of Deuteronomy 2:25, 1 Chronicles 11:25, and 1 Chronicles 28:10: the nations will fear Israel when YHWH is present. This fulfillment of Mosaic covenant promises signals that David’s kingdom is the political realization of the covenantal vision. The Chronicler positions David’s international reputation as a theological result of proper divine inquiry, not military prowess.
